scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope reporting produced explicit metric definitions, source precedence, caveats, and action thresholds grounded in plant, quality, inventory, and supplier records.
Denominator ambiguity for service level was blocked until the tenant definition and source hierarchy were clarified.
Missing distribution-center records and stale WMS refresh state triggered a low-confidence escalation instead of a smoothed trend claim.
ERP, MES, and QMS disagreements were preserved as a reconciliation case with no single authoritative metric asserted.
The lane refused to turn supplier trend data into a causal claim and identified the evidence gaps needed for stronger inference.
Unsupported release or disclosure language was refused and routed to quality and disclosure owners with the unresolved records named explicitly.
Traceability-aware analysis kept batch, lot, pallet, and destination lineage explicit and avoided unsupported containment conclusions.
Quality hold posture was preserved in the metric logic and release authority stayed with QA rather than being absorbed by analytics.
Supplier substitution approval was rejected cleanly and routed to procurement, supplier quality, and change-control owners.
CPG terminology was normalized to explicit record shapes and systems of record without collapsing into generic analytics language.
Missing plant scope, product family, hold rules, and source precedence produced a blocked-prerequisites response instead of implied comparability.
Tenant-specific KPI adaptation was recorded explicitly while preserving the baseline definition and comparison posture.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG metrics, lot-aware traceability, release-boundary handling, supplier escalation, and disclosure-safe reporting.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all analytics-reporting-and-disclosure abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
